section.title: A Wedding Song for the King
section.sectionRationale: Titled 'a love song' in its superscription, this royal wedding psalm addresses the Davidic king in language so exalted, his throne is called God's own, verse 6, that the author of Hebrews applies it directly to Christ, Hebrews 1:8-9, making it one of the clearest messianic psalms in the Psalter.
section.thesis: This wedding song praises the ideal king, righteous, victorious, and anointed above his companions, in terms no merely human king could fully bear, terms the New Testament recognizes as belonging finally to Christ, the eternal King whose throne is forever and whose bride, the church, is called to undivided devotion.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the poet's inspired theme, verses 1-2; the summons to the warrior king, verses 3-5; the king's eternal, God-called throne and anointing, verses 6-9; the address to the bride, verses 10-15; and the promise of enduring dynasty and fame, verses 16-17.

units.label: My heart is stirred by a noble theme

verses.text:
My heart is stirred by a noble theme as I recite my verses to the king; 
my tongue is the pen of a skillful writer.

You are the most handsome of men; grace has anointed your lips, 
since God has blessed you forever.

units.label: Strap your sword at your side, O mighty warrior
units.note: the summons to the warrior king

verses.text: Strap your sword at your side, O mighty warrior; appear in your majesty and splendor.

In your splendor ride forth in victory on behalf of truth and humility and justice; 
may your right hand show your awesome deeds.
Your arrows pierce the hearts of the king's foes; the nations fall beneath your feet.

units.label: Your throne, O God, endures forever and ever
units.note: the king's eternal, God-called throne and anointing

verses.text: Your throne, O {{elohim|God}}, endures forever and ever, and justice is the scepter of Your kingdom.
You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness; therefore God, your God, has anointed you above your companions with the oil of {{sasson|joy}}.

All your garments are fragrant with myrrh and aloes and cassia; 
from palaces of ivory the harps make you glad.

The daughters of kings are among your honored women; the queen stands at your right hand, 
adorned with the gold of Ophir.

units.label: Listen, O daughter, forget your people

verses.text: Listen, O daughter! Consider and incline your ear: Forget your people and your father's house,
and the king will desire your beauty; bow to him, for he is your lord.
The Daughter of Tyre will come with a gift; men of wealth will seek your favor.
All glorious is the princess in her chamber; her gown is embroidered with gold.
In colorful garments she is led to the king; her virgin companions are brought before you.
They are led in with joy and gladness; they enter the palace of the king.

units.label: I will commemorate your name through all generations
units.note: the promise of enduring dynasty and fame

verses.text: Your sons will succeed your fathers; you will make them princes throughout the land.
I will commemorate your name through all generations; therefore the nations will praise you forever and ever.

elohim.gloss: God. The psalmist astonishingly addresses the king's throne with this title, language no merely human king could rightly bear without qualification. The writer of Hebrews quotes this very line as the Father's own address to the Son, Hebrews 1:8.

sasson.gloss: Joy or gladness, especially festive joy. The king is anointed with this oil above his companions, language Hebrews 1:9 applies to Christ, anointed with the oil of gladness beyond His companions.

basic.context: Titled a love song, this psalm was composed for a royal wedding, praising the Davidic king's righteousness, victory, and splendor, then addressing his bride with counsel to leave her own people and cling wholly to him. Its language becomes so exalted, calling the king's throne God's own throne, that it points beyond any merely human king.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm functioned first as a real celebration for a historical royal wedding, yet its language so exceeds what any human king could bear on his own, especially verse 6, that Scripture itself, in Hebrews 1, reads it as ultimately fulfilled in Christ, the true and greater King.
basic.originalLanguages: The king's throne is called elohim, God, verse 6, astonishing language quoted directly by Hebrews as the Father's address to the Son. He is anointed with the oil of sasson, joy, above his companions, verse 7, language Hebrews 1:9 also applies to Christ's unique anointing above all others.
basic.typology: The king's eternal throne, called God's own, and his anointing above his companions with the oil of joy point forward to Christ, the true King whose throne is truly and eternally divine and whose anointing as Messiah exceeds every other. The bride summoned to leave her people for the king anticipates the church, the bride of Christ.
basic.christ: Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes Psalm 45:6-7 directly as words the Father speaks to the Son, proof of Christ's full deity and eternal reign. The whole psalm's picture of a righteous, victorious, radiant King wedded to a devoted bride finds its truest fulfillment in Christ and His church.
basic.god: God is shown anointing and establishing the king's throne, blessing him forever, and Himself being the one whose own throne the king's throne images, so that the king's righteous reign becomes a window onto God's own eternal, just kingship.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes this psalm directly as the Father's address to the Son. Ephesians 5:25-32 describes Christ's love for His bride, the church, in similarly devoted terms. Revelation 19:7-9 pictures the marriage supper of the Lamb, the wedding this psalm anticipates.
basic.oneStory: This royal wedding song, praising an anointed king whose throne is called God's own, points beyond any human king to Christ, the eternal King and Bridegroom, whose bride, the church, is called to forsake every lesser loyalty for undivided devotion to Him.

context.literary: A royal wedding psalm, titled 'a love song,' structured as alternating addresses to the bridegroom, verses 2-9, and 16-17, and to the bride, verses 10-15, framed by the poet's own inspired introduction, verses 1-2.
context.historical: Likely composed for the wedding of a Davidic king, perhaps Solomon given the reference to the gold of Ophir and ivory palaces, though the psalm's language of the king's throne as God's own exceeds any merely historical occasion.
context.canonical: One of the clearest messianic psalms, its address to the king's throne as elohim quoted directly in Hebrews 1:8-9 as proof of the Son's full deity, making this wedding song a foundational text for the church's confession of Christ's divine kingship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To celebrate a Davidic royal wedding in the most exalted terms available, praising the king's righteousness, victory, and splendor and instructing the bride toward undivided devotion, language that Scripture itself later reveals points beyond any human king to the Messiah.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific royal wedding celebration; it prescribes as an abiding pattern wholehearted devotion to the true King, Christ, and models the bride's call to forsake every lesser loyalty for her Bridegroom.

debates.question: Is Psalm 45 originally about a historical Israelite king's wedding, or messianic prophecy from the very start?

views.label: The psalm was composed as a real court poem for a historical royal wedding, whose idealized language of kingship the New Testament recognizes as finding its full truth only in Christ
views.case: The psalm's specific details, ivory palaces, the gold of Ophir, the Daughter of Tyre bringing gifts, fit a real historical occasion, most plausibly a Solomonic wedding, suggesting an original historical setting later read messianically.

views.label: The psalm was directly intended as messianic prophecy from the start, since no mere human king's throne could rightly be called God without careful qualification
views.case: Verse 6's direct address, 'Your throne, O God,' is so theologically weighty that some argue the Spirit intended the ultimate referent to be the Messiah alone, with any historical king serving merely as an imperfect occasion for words that exceed him.

debates.bearing: Both views agree that Hebrews 1 is right to apply this psalm to Christ; the question is whether that application is a fulfillment and escalation of real historical language or the psalm's sole original intent, but either way its final, truest referent is Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The king's throne is addressed with the title elohim, God, verse 6, language Hebrews 1:8 quotes directly as the Father speaking to the Son. His anointing is with the oil of sasson, joy, verse 7, above his companions, language Hebrews 1:9 applies to Christ's anointing beyond all others.

typology.type: The king's throne addressed as God's own throne, enduring forever, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Applied directly to Christ, the Son whose throne truly is forever, Hebrews 1:8-9.

typology.type: The king anointed with the oil of joy above his companions, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Christ, anointed above all His fellows, Hebrews 1:9, the perfect and final fulfillment of kingly anointing.

typology.type: The bride summoned to leave her people and cling wholly to the king, verses 10-11
typology.fulfillment: The church, the bride of Christ, called to forsake every lesser loyalty for singular devotion to her Bridegroom, Ephesians 5:25-32, Revelation 19:7-9.

study.christ: The author of Hebrews quotes Psalm 45:6-7 directly as words the Father speaks to the Son, 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever' and 'God, Your God, has anointed You with the oil of gladness beyond Your companions,' Hebrews 1:8-9, offered as scriptural proof of Christ's full deity and eternal, righteous reign. The wedding imagery of the whole psalm anticipates Christ as the Bridegroom of His church.
study.god: God is the one who blesses and anoints the king forever, and whose own throne the king's throne images so exactly that Scripture can address the king directly as God, revealing that true, righteous kingship is ultimately God's own kingship, fulfilled completely in Christ.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Psalm 45:6-7 directly as the Father's address to the Son, proof of Christ's deity and eternal throne.
Describes Christ's devoted love for His bride, the church, echoing this psalm's wedding imagery.
Pictures the marriage supper of the Lamb, the wedding feast this psalm's royal wedding anticipates.

study.oneStory: This ancient wedding song for a Davidic king, addressed in terms so exalted that his throne is called God's own, points beyond any human king to Christ, the eternal King and Bridegroom, whose bride, the church, is summoned to forsake every lesser loyalty for undivided devotion to Him.

quiz.q: What does the poet compare his own tongue to in verse 1?

options.text: The pen of a skillful writer.
options.note: The poet describes his inspired composition using the image of a skilled scribe's pen.

options.text: A sharpened sword.
options.note: The sword imagery belongs to the king in verse 3, not to the poet's tongue.

options.text: A harp played in the palace.
options.note: Harps appear later, verse 8, describing the king's palace, not the poet's own tongue.

options.text: A trumpet announcing victory.
options.note: No trumpet appears in this psalm; the specific image for the poet's tongue is a skillful writer's pen.

quiz.q: What is the king summoned to do in verses 3-5?

options.text: Strap on his sword and ride forth in majesty and victory for truth and justice.
options.note: The king is called as a mighty warrior to ride out triumphantly on behalf of truth, humility, and justice.

options.text: Lay down his weapons and seek peace with all nations.
options.note: The text calls him to arm himself and ride forth in victory, not to disarm.

options.text: Retreat to his palace and avoid conflict.
options.note: The summons is to active, victorious battle, not retreat.

options.text: Send his armies ahead while he remains behind.
options.note: The king himself is called to ride forth personally in splendor and victory.

quiz.q: How does verse 6 address the king's throne, language later quoted in Hebrews 1:8-9?

options.text: "Your throne, O God, endures forever and ever."
options.note: This direct address of the throne as God's own is quoted by Hebrews as the Father's words to the Son.

options.text: "Your throne will one day pass to your enemies."
options.note: The verse promises the throne endures forever, the opposite of passing to enemies.

options.text: "Your throne is like the thrones of other kings."
options.note: The verse's point is precisely that this throne is unlike other kings' thrones, called God's own.

options.text: "Your throne will be established by your armies."
options.note: The text credits God, not military strength, with establishing and sustaining the throne forever.

quiz.q: According to verses 10-11, what is the bride called to do?

options.text: Forget her people and father's house, and incline her ear to the king.
options.note: She is called to undivided devotion to the king, forsaking her former household loyalty.

options.text: Return quickly to her own family and homeland.
options.note: The text calls her to forget, not return to, her people and father's house.

options.text: Remain equally loyal to both her family and the king.
options.note: The summons is to singular devotion to the king, not divided loyalty.

options.text: Bring her own army to help the king in battle.
options.note: The bride's calling here is devotion and honor to the king, not military assistance.

quiz.q: What does the New Testament book of Hebrews do with Psalm 45:6-7?

options.text: It quotes the verses as words the Father speaks to the Son, proof of Christ's deity and eternal throne.
options.note: Hebrews 1:8-9 applies this royal wedding language directly to Christ as evidence of His full divinity.

options.text: It dismisses the verses as merely poetic exaggeration about a human king.
options.note: Hebrews treats the verses with full theological seriousness as address to the Son, not as mere exaggeration.

options.text: It applies the verses only to angels, not to Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 1 actually contrasts Christ with the angels, applying this psalm's throne language to the Son specifically.

options.text: It reinterprets the verses as referring to the future church, not any king.
options.note: Hebrews applies the verses to Christ the King Himself, not directly to the church as its referent.

questions: This wedding song describes a king whose righteousness, victory, and splendor exceed any mere human ruler. What does it stir in you to know that Christ, the true King this psalm ultimately describes, reigns in perfect righteousness forever?

The bride is called to forget her people and father's house for undivided devotion to the king. What lesser loyalties competes most for the devotion Christ, your true Bridegroom, calls you to give Him alone?
Hebrews quotes this psalm as the Father's own address to the Son, calling Him God. How does meditating on Christ's full deity change the way you approach Him in prayer and worship?

The king is praised for loving righteousness and hating wickedness, verse 7. Where in your life is this pattern, love for what is right and hatred for what is wrong, weakest right now, and what would it look like to grow in it?
This psalm anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19. How does picturing the church as Christ's beloved, honored bride shape how you see your own place in His story?

translationNotes.note: My heart is stirred renders a Hebrew verb picturing a bubbling over or overflowing, describing the poet's inspiration pouring out into composed verse.
Your throne, O God has been read either as a direct vocative address to the king as God, or as a description, your divine throne; Hebrews 1:8 reads it as direct address, applying it to the Son.
The gold of Ophir was proverbial in Scripture for the finest, most valuable gold, 1 Kings 9:28, underscoring the queen's supreme honor at the king's right hand.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 45:1-17. No em or en dashes present. Searched gospelinlife.com, truthforlife.org, desiringgod.org, and thegospelcoalition.org this session for a dedicated Psalm 45 sermon from the trusted cluster; none found, so sermon status is none.
